item.title: “Monks, what is the origination of form? What is the origination of feeling? What is the origination of perception? What is the origination of formations?…
item.description:
“Monks, what is the origination of form? What is the origination of feeling? What is the origination of perception? What is the origination of formations? What is the origination of consciousness? Here, monks, a monk delights, welcomes, and remains attached. What does he delight in, welcome, and remain attached to? He delights, welcomes, and remains attached to form. Because of his delight, welcome, and attachment to form, delight in form arises. Because of delight in form, clinging to form arises. With clinging to form as condition, becoming arises; with becoming as condition, birth arises; with birth as condition, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise. Such is the origination of this whole mass of suffering.

He delights, welcomes, and remains attached to feeling … perception … formations … consciousness. Because of his delight, welcome, and attachment to consciousness, delight in consciousness arises. Because of delight in consciousness, clinging to consciousness arises … Such is the origination of this whole mass of suffering. This, monks, is the origination of form, the origination of feeling, the origination of perception, the origination of formations, the origination of consciousness.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.5
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Through lust for form, monks, consciousness becomes established with a foothold. With form as its object, with form as its support, seeking delight, it grows,…
item.description:
“Through lust for form, monks, consciousness becomes established with a foothold. With form as its object, with form as its support, seeking delight, it grows, increases, and expands. Through lust for feeling … for perception … for formations, monks, consciousness becomes established with a foothold. With formations as its object, with formations as its support, seeking delight, it grows, increases, and expands. Monks, if anyone says: ‘Apart from form, apart from feeling, apart from perception, apart from formations, I will show the coming and going, the passing away and arising, the growth, increase, and expansion of consciousness’ — such a thing is not possible. Monks, if a monk has abandoned lust for the form element — with the abandonment of lust, the basis is cut off, the support of consciousness does not exist. Monks, if he has abandoned lust for the feeling element … for the perception element … for the formation element … for the consciousness element — with the abandonment of lust, the basis is cut off, the support of consciousness does not exist. Without that support, consciousness does not expand, does not generate activity, and is liberated; being liberated, it is steady; being steady, it is content; being content, it is not agitated; being not agitated, he personally attains Nibbāna. He knows: ‘Birth is destroyed, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.54
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “‘A being, a being,’ venerable sir, is said. To what extent, venerable sir, is one called a being?” - “Rādha, whatever desire, lust, delight, craving…
item.description:
“‘A being, a being,’ venerable sir, is said. To what extent, venerable sir, is one called a being?” - “Rādha, whatever desire, lust, delight, craving there is for form — entanglement there, immersion there — thus one is called a being. For feeling … perception … formations … for consciousness — entanglement there, immersion there — thus one is called a being.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 23.2
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, these ten perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, having the deathless as their end. What…
item.description:
“Monks, these ten perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, having the deathless as their end. What ten? The perception of foulness, the perception of death, the perception of disgust with food, the perception of non-delight in the entire world, the perception of impermanence, the perception of suffering in impermanence, the perception of not-self in suffering, the perception of abandoning, the perception of dispassion, the perception of cessation. These ten perceptions, monks, when developed and cultivated, are of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, having the deathless as their end.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 10.56
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, this body of the fool is generated. That ignorance the fool has not abandoned. That craving the…
item.description:
“Monks, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, this body of the fool is generated. That ignorance the fool has not abandoned. That craving the fool has not destroyed. Why is that? Monks, the fool does not live the holy life for the complete destruction of suffering. Therefore, with the breakup of the body, after death, the fool goes to another body. Having gone to another body, he is not freed from birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair. I say that he is not freed from suffering.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 12.19
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - “Venerable Sāriputta, what things should a virtuous monk attend to wisely?” - “Venerable Koṭṭhika, a virtuous monk should attend wisely to the five aggregates…
item.description:
- “Venerable Sāriputta, what things should a virtuous monk attend to wisely?” - “Venerable Koṭṭhika, a virtuous monk should attend wisely to the five aggregates of clinging as impermanent, as suffering, as a disease, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self. What five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling aggregate of clinging, the perception aggregate of clinging, the formation aggregate of clinging, the consciousness aggregate of clinging. Venerable Koṭṭhika, a virtuous monk should attend wisely to these five aggregates of clinging as impermanent … as not-self.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.122
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “I do not see any other form, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a woman as the form of a man. The…
item.description:
“I do not see any other form, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a woman as the form of a man. The form of a man, monks, obsesses and occupies the mind of a woman.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 1.6
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Again, the five kinds of kings Are present in the world — Learn to subdue them, Always keeping mindfulness. Toward forms and sounds, Toward tastes…
item.description:
“Again, the five kinds of kings
Are present in the world —
Learn to subdue them,
Always keeping mindfulness.
Toward forms and sounds,
Toward tastes and smells,
And toward contacts —
Subdue desire and lust.

Subdue desire
For these things.
The mindful monk,
With mind well liberated,
Then, at the right time,
Rightly reflecting on the Dhamma,
With unified mind,
He should destroy darkness.” So spoke the Blessed One.

From: Sutta Nipāta 4.16
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Raṭṭhapāla: Behold this decorated image — A heap of wounds, a collection of sores, Diseased, full of lustful thoughts, Nothing stable or permanent here.…
item.description:
Elder Raṭṭhapāla:

Behold this decorated image —
A heap of wounds, a collection of sores,
Diseased, full of lustful thoughts,
Nothing stable or permanent here.

Behold this adorned appearance,
With jewels and earrings —
A skeleton covered by skin,
Made beautiful by clothes.

The feet are painted with lac and dye,
The face is smeared with powder and paste —
Enough to fool a fool,
Not one who seeks enlightenment.

The hair is curled in eight rings,
The eyes are rubbed with collyrium and rouge —
Enough to fool a fool,
Not one who seeks enlightenment.

Like a newly painted medicine box,
This foul body is adorned —
Enough to fool a fool,
Not one who seeks enlightenment.

The hunter set his trap and snare —
The wise deer does not touch the snare.
Having eaten the bait, we go away,
Leaving the trapper to mourn.

The hunter’s trap is cut —
The wise deer does not touch the snare.
Having eaten the bait, we go away,
Leaving the trapper to grieve.

From: Theragāthā 16.4
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by…
item.description:
“Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving. Monks, suppose a dog was tied by a leash to a strong post or pillar. If it walks, it walks close to that post or pillar. If it stands, it stands close to that post or pillar. If it sits, it sits close to that post or pillar. If it lies down, it lies down close to that post or pillar. So too, monks, an untaught ordinary person regards form as: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self.’ He regards feeling … perception … formations … consciousness as: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self.’ If he walks, he walks close to these five aggregates of clinging. If he stands, he stands close to them. If he sits, he sits close to them. If he lies down, he lies close to them. Therefore, monks, one should constantly reflect on one’s own mind thus: ‘For a long time, this mind has been defiled by greed, hatred, and delusion. Through the defilement of the mind, beings are defiled. Through the purification of the mind, beings are purified.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.100
📖 Read this sutta
